findings.description: WebSocket subscription selector header parsed as SpEL expression - user-controlled input flows into SpelExpressionParser.parseExpression(), then evaluated with SimpleEvaluationContext
findings.reasoning: The `selector` header value from WebSocket SUBSCRIBE messages is parsed directly as a SpEL expression via `SpelExpressionParser.parseExpression(selector)`. This is user-controlled input flowing into SpEL parsing. When the expression is later evaluated at line 226, it uses `SimpleEvaluationContext.forPropertyAccessors(new SimpMessageHeaderPropertyAccessor()).build()` which restricts method invocations and type references. However, `SimpleEvaluationContext` still allows property access and some operations. The parsing itself could still cause parsing-level issues (DoS via complex expressions), and there may be edge cases in the SimpleEvaluationContext restriction. This is the CVE-2018-1270 pattern - the fix (SimpleEvaluationContext) appears to be applied here, so full RCE is unlikely, but the pattern warrants review.

evidence.notes: SimpleEvaluationContext restricts type references (T(java.lang.Runtime)), static method access, and constructor invocations. It does allow property access and simple comparisons. CVE-2018-1270 (which was RCE) was fixed by switching to SimpleEvaluationContext. The current code appears to have the fix applied.

findings.description: WebSocket subscription selector header parsed as SpEL expression from user-controlled input and evaluated
findings.reasoning: The `selector` header from a WebSocket SUBSCRIBE message is directly parsed as a SpEL expression using `SpelExpressionParser`. While the evaluation at line 226 uses `SimpleEvaluationContext.forPropertyAccessors(new SimpMessageHeaderPropertyAccessor())` which is restricted (no type references, constructors, or arbitrary method invocations), the parsing step itself happens on raw user input. This is the pattern exploited in CVE-2018-1270. The mitigated version uses SimpleEvaluationContext, but the restriction effectiveness depends on the SpEL engine version and whether property accessors can be chained to reach sensitive operations. The user input flows directly from the WebSocket SUBSCRIBE message's "selector" header into SpEL parsing.

evidence.mitigations: SimpleEvaluationContext is used which restricts type references, constructors, and method invocations. However user input is still parsed as SpEL with SpelExpressionParser - this was the CVE-2018-1270 pattern that was partially mitigated.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a WebSocket STOMP SUBSCRIBE message with a crafted 'selector' header containing SpEL expression that gets parsed and evaluated against message headers
exploit_payload.prerequisites: WebSocket endpoint using STOMP with DefaultSubscriptionRegistry enabled (default for Spring WebSocket)

findings.description: DefaultDeserializer uses ConfigurableObjectInputStream without class allowlisting, enabling potential unsafe deserialization
findings.code_snippet: ObjectInputStream objectInputStream = new ConfigurableObjectInputStream(inputStream, this.classLoader);
findings.reasoning: DefaultDeserializer creates a ConfigurableObjectInputStream and calls readObject() without any class filtering (no allowlist or denylist). ConfigurableObjectInputStream only resolves classes using a specified ClassLoader - it has no class-level security filtering. The resolveClass() method uses ClassUtils.forName() which will load any class from the classpath. If this deserializer is exposed to untrusted input (e.g., via remoting, messaging, or HTTP endpoints), it could be exploited with gadget chains. This is a utility class used by DeserializingConverter and SerializationDelegate, which could be wired into HTTP/messaging pipelines. Exploitability depends on whether gadget chain libraries (Commons Collections, Spring Beans, etc.) are on the classpath. Needs manual review to confirm actual exposure to untrusted data.

evidence.analysis: ConfigurableObjectInputStream has no class filtering - its resolveClass() only uses a custom ClassLoader but does not restrict which classes can be deserialized. No allowlist or denylist is implemented. The class is used by DeserializingConverter (converts byte[] to Object) which could be used in Spring Integration, Spring Data Redis, or other data pipelines that process untrusted input.

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in CacheOperationExpressionEvaluator - developer-controlled annotation expressions
findings.reasoning: The SpEL expressions evaluated here come from Spring's @Cacheable, @CacheEvict, and @CachePut annotation attributes (keyExpression, conditionExpression, unlessExpression). These expressions are defined by developers in Java annotations at compile time, not by end users at runtime. There is no pathway for user-controlled input to reach these expression evaluation points. This is standard Spring Cache functionality operating as designed.

evidence.code_context: The expressions evaluated here (keyExpression, conditionExpression, unlessExpression) are strings derived from @Cacheable/@CacheEvict/@CachePut annotation attributes. These are hardcoded by developers in Java source annotations and cannot be modified by end users at runtime.
evidence.notes: Standard Spring Cache SpEL evaluation - developer-controlled expressions from annotations. No user input pathway.

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in CacheOperationExpressionEvaluator - developer-controlled via @Cacheable annotations
findings.reasoning: The conditionExpression comes from Spring's @Cacheable(condition="...") annotations, which are developer-controlled. These are not user-controllable at runtime. The expression strings are fixed at compile-time in annotation values. This is a false positive - there is no user input path to this SpEL evaluation.

evidence.code_context: Expression evaluated in CacheOperationExpressionEvaluator.condition() which is called from Spring's caching AOP infrastructure. The conditionExpression parameter originates from @Cacheable(condition='...') annotation values, which are set by developers in source code and cannot be modified by end users at runtime.
evidence.notes: False positive: expression source is developer-controlled annotation values

findings.description: SpEL expression evaluation in CacheOperationExpressionEvaluator - developer-controlled via annotations
findings.reasoning: The SpEL expression evaluated here originates from the `unless` attribute of `@CacheEvict` or `@Cacheable` annotations, which are developer-controlled code constructs. There is no path for user-controlled input to reach this evaluation point as an expression string.

evidence.code_context: The unlessExpression parameter comes from @CacheEvict/@Cacheable annotation attributes which are static developer-defined strings. The evaluation context is method-based, not user-input based.

findings.description: SpEL expression evaluation in EventExpressionEvaluator - developer-controlled via @EventListener annotations
findings.code_snippet:
return (Boolean.TRUE.equals(getExpression(this.conditionCache, methodKey, conditionExpression).getValue(
				evaluationContext, Boolean.class)));
findings.reasoning: The conditionExpression originates from @EventListener annotation's condition attribute, which is a developer-controlled compile-time constant. No user input can influence the expression string itself.

evidence.code_context: The conditionExpression parameter comes from @EventListener(condition='...') annotation attributes which are static developer-defined strings compiled into bytecode.

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in StandardBeanExpressionResolver - developer-controlled Spring bean config expressions
findings.code_snippet: return expr.getValue(sec);
findings.reasoning: The StandardBeanExpressionResolver evaluates SpEL expressions from Spring bean definitions (#{...} syntax in XML or annotations). These expressions originate from developer-controlled configuration files, not user input. While StandardEvaluationContext is used (full power), the expressions are from trusted sources (application configuration). Not exploitable by external users unless they can modify Spring configuration files, which would require a different attack vector entirely.

evidence.code_context: The value parameter comes from Spring's bean definition processing pipeline, not user HTTP requests. The expression is parsed from #{...} placeholders in @Value annotations or XML bean definitions.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Expression source is Spring bean definitions (XML/annotations) - developer-controlled. Not reachable from user input.
evidence.notes: False positive - no user input path to this evaluation point.

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in CompositeStringExpression - internal SpEL infrastructure, not user input
findings.code_snippet: String value = expression.getValue(String.class);
findings.reasoning: CompositeStringExpression is an internal SpEL implementation class that combines multiple sub-expressions. The getValue() calls here are part of SpEL's own infrastructure for evaluating composite (template) expressions. The expressions themselves are parsed from developer-controlled sources (Spring configuration, annotations). This is not a point where user input enters the evaluation pipeline.

evidence.code_context: CompositeStringExpression iterates over pre-parsed sub-expressions and calls getValue() on each. The expressions are parsed upstream by SpelExpressionParser and stored as an array.
evidence.notes: This is internal SpEL infrastructure - false positive.

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in LiteralExpression - returns literal string value, no user input injection
findings.code_snippet: public String getValue() throws EvaluationException { return this.literalValue; }
findings.reasoning: LiteralExpression.getValue() simply returns the literal string value it was constructed with. This is not an expression evaluation that could execute arbitrary code - it's just a wrapper around a constant string. No user-controlled SpEL evaluation occurs here.

evidence.code_context: LiteralExpression simply returns a stored literal value string - there is no actual expression evaluation happening. It implements the Expression interface but returns a constant.
evidence.notes: False positive - no SpEL evaluation risk here

findings.description: SpEL evaluation in EvalTag JSP tag - developer-controlled expression, not user input
findings.reasoning: The EvalTag JSP tag evaluates SpEL expressions set via the `setExpression()` method, which receives the expression from JSP tag attributes defined in developer-authored JSP templates. The expression string is not directly user-controlled - it comes from the JSP tag attribute in server-side template code. While StandardEvaluationContext is used (full power), the expression source is the developer/template author, not end-user input. This is a false positive for remote user-controlled SpEL injection.

evidence.code_context: setExpression() called from JSP tag attributes defined in developer JSP templates. The expression is parsed at tag setup time from the JSP tag attribute value, not from HTTP request parameters. StandardEvaluationContext is used but expression origin is developer-controlled.
evidence.notes: False positive - expression source is JSP developer-controlled template attribute, not end-user request data.

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization in HttpInvokerServiceExporter: HTTP request body deserialized via ObjectInputStream without class filtering
findings.reasoning: The HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from request.getInputStream() and passes it to readRemoteInvocation() -> doReadRemoteInvocation() which calls ois.readObject() without any type filtering. The ObjectInputStream is created from the raw HTTP request body via CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream. There is no ObjectInputFilter or resolveClass() override to restrict which classes can be deserialized. An attacker can send a crafted serialized payload using gadget chains (Commons Collections, Spring Beans, etc.) to achieve RCE. The code itself has a WARNING comment: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject(). The code comment in HttpInvokerServiceExporter explicitly warns: 'Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step.'
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> request.getInputStream() -> CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream -> ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no class filtering
evidence.notes: No ObjectInputFilter applied. CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream does not restrict which classes can be deserialized - it only handles codebase URLs for RMI-style class loading.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted Java serialized payload (e.g., Commons Collections gadget chain) in the HTTP request body to execute arbitrary OS commands on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the HTTP invoker endpoint (no authentication required by default)

findings.description: Unsafe deserialization via ObjectInputStream in AbstractHttpInvokerRequestExecutor - HTTP response body deserialized without class filtering
findings.code_snippet: Object obj = ois.readObject();
findings.reasoning: The AbstractHttpInvokerRequestExecutor.doReadRemoteInvocationResult() method calls ois.readObject() on an ObjectInputStream created from an HTTP response body (via createObjectInputStream()). While this is on the client side reading server responses, if the server is compromised or a MITM attack is possible, the deserialized content could trigger arbitrary code execution via gadget chains. No ObjectInputFilter is applied to restrict deserialized types. The CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream does not restrict what classes can be loaded - it only handles codebase URLs for dynamic class loading from remote locations.

evidence.code_context: protected RemoteInvocationResult doReadRemoteInvocationResult(ObjectInputStream ois) throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException { Object obj = ois.readObject(); if (!(obj instanceof RemoteInvocationResult)) { throw new RemoteException(...); } return (RemoteInvocationResult) obj; } - The stream comes from HTTP response body, no class filtering applied.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP response stream -> createObjectInputStream(CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream) -> doReadRemoteInvocationResult -> ois.readObject() with no type filter

exploit_payload.scenario: MITM attacker intercepts HTTP invoker response and replaces it with a serialized gadget chain payload to achieve remote code execution on the client
exploit_payload.prerequisites: MITM position or compromised server; gadget chain library (Commons Collections 3.x/4.x) on classpath

findings.description: Unsafe deserialization in DefaultDeserializer with no class filtering
findings.reasoning: DefaultDeserializer.deserialize() wraps any InputStream in a ConfigurableObjectInputStream and calls readObject() with no class filtering. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only uses a custom classloader but does not apply any ObjectInputFilter or allowlist. This class is a general-purpose utility exposed via Spring's Deserializer interface that can be configured into Spring Integration message channels, HTTP message converters, or other components that process external data. If an attacker can supply a malicious serialized payload to any component using this deserializer, they can exploit gadget chains.

evidence.code_context: ConfigurableObjectInputStream is used but provides no class filtering - only custom classloader support. No ObjectInputFilter is applied. The deserializer accepts arbitrary InputStream sources.
evidence.notes: Used as a general Spring Deserializer - could be wired to network streams in Spring Integration or similar

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a malicious serialized payload to any endpoint backed by DefaultDeserializer
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Commons Collections or other gadget chain library must be on classpath; DefaultDeserializer must be wired to a network-accessible stream
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via gadget chain

findings.description: Unsafe deserialization in SerializationUtils.deserialize() without class filtering
findings.reasoning: SerializationUtils.deserialize() uses a plain ObjectInputStream without any class filtering or type allowlisting. It is used in CacheResultInterceptor to deserialize exceptions stored in JCache. If byte arrays from an external or untrusted source (e.g., externalized cache like Redis, Memcached) are passed to this method, gadget chains could be exploited for RCE. There is no ObjectInputFilter or resolveClass() override that would restrict allowed types.

evidence.usage_context: Used in CacheResultInterceptor.java:153: SerializationUtils.deserialize(SerializationUtils.serialize(exception)) - serializes then immediately deserializes, but the general utility is public API and could be called with externally-sourced bytes.
evidence.notes: No ObjectInputFilter, no resolveClass override, no allowlist applied.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a malicious serialized payload (e.g., via externalized cache) to SerializationUtils.deserialize(), triggering gadget chain execution
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control byte array input to SerializationUtils.deserialize(); Commons Collections or other gadget chain library on classpath
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via unsafe deserialization

findings.description: Unsafe deserialization in test fixture - not production code
findings.code_snippet:
ObjectInputStream objectInputStream = new ObjectInputStream(inputStream);
return (T) objectInputStream.readObject();
findings.reasoning: This is in a test fixture file (testFixtures directory), used for testing purposes only. It's not production code that can be reached by external requests. Test utilities that deserialize objects are expected to do so without filtering since they're testing serialization round-trips.

evidence.code_context: File is in src/testFixtures directory - test code only, not production. Used for testing serialization round-trips.
evidence.notes: Test fixtures are not deployed to production environments and are not reachable by external attackers.

findings.description: TransactionInterceptor readObject uses Java serialization for its own object state - internal controlled data only
findings.reasoning: The readObject method in TransactionInterceptor is a custom serialization handler for the class itself. It reads back fields that were previously written by writeObject. This is standard Java serialization for the class's own state. The data being read is from the JVM's own serialized state, not from an untrusted network input. The class implements Serializable to support use in distributed environments, but the readObject method reads back its own serialized fields. This is a false positive because there is no code path where external/untrusted bytes are fed into TransactionInterceptor's serialization methods directly.

evidence.code_context: The TransactionInterceptor.readObject() reads back fields (transactionManagerBeanName, transactionManager, transactionAttributeSource, beanFactory) that were written by the corresponding writeObject() method. This is a standard custom serialization pattern. The data source is the object's own serialized state, not untrusted network input.
evidence.notes: This is part of Java's serialization mechanism for the class itself, not a deserialization of external untrusted data.

findings.description: ScriptEngine.eval() on server-configured template files - not user-controlled
findings.code_snippet: html = engine.eval(template, bindings);
findings.reasoning: The ScriptTemplateView evaluates templates loaded from server-side resource files (configured by the application developer), not from user HTTP input. The `getTemplate(url)` method loads from a server-side resource path. The model data (user-influenced) is passed as bindings/data, not as script code. This is the intended use of a template engine where the template files are server-controlled. This is not a code injection vulnerability from user input.

evidence.code_context: template is loaded via getTemplate(url) which reads from a server-side Resource path. User input goes into model bindings, not script code. The eval() call evaluates the template content which comes from the application's configured template files.
evidence.notes: False positive - template content is from server-side files, not user-controlled HTTP input.

findings.description: ScriptEngine.eval() in StandardScriptEvaluator evaluates script content from ScriptSource - content is loaded from server-side resources, not user HTTP input
findings.code_snippet: return engine.eval(script.getScriptAsString());
findings.reasoning: The StandardScriptEvaluator evaluates script content from ScriptSource objects. The ScriptSource is a Spring abstraction that loads script content from server-side resources (files, classpath). There is no direct path from HTTP user input to this eval call - the script content is loaded from trusted server-side resources configured by the application, not from user-controlled HTTP request parameters.

evidence.code_context: StandardScriptEvaluator.evaluate() calls engine.eval(script.getScriptAsString()) where script is a ScriptSource abstraction loaded from server-side resources like ResourceScriptSource, not from HTTP user input.
evidence.notes: Script content originates from server-side resources, not user-controlled HTTP input. False positive.

findings.description: TransactionInterceptor readObject - internal Java serialization for custom serialization support, not exposed to untrusted input
findings.reasoning: TransactionInterceptor implements custom Java serialization via readObject/writeObject methods to handle its superclass fields. The ObjectInputStream.readObject() calls here are part of Java's standard deserialization mechanism for a Spring AOP interceptor class. The data being deserialized is only the object's own state during normal Java serialization lifecycle, not external/HTTP data. This is not exploitable by remote attackers.

evidence.notes: This is a private readObject method used for Java serialization lifecycle. It handles deserialization of the object's own state. Not exposed to untrusted HTTP input directly.

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker server-side handler - ObjectInputStream.readObject() called on untrusted HTTP request body without type filtering
findings.reasoning:
The `doReadRemoteInvocation` method calls `ois.readObject()` on an `ObjectInputStream` created from the HTTP request body (via `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.readRemoteInvocation()` → `request.getInputStream()`). 

The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check occurs AFTER deserialization completes, so any gadget chain embedded in the serialized payload executes during `readObject()` before the type check. The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream` chain does NOT implement any `ObjectInputFilter` (Java 9+ serial filter) or override `resolveClass()` to restrict types to an allowlist.

The attack surface is: any HTTP client that can POST to the Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint can send a malicious Java serialized payload containing a gadget chain (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Commons BeanUtils, Spring Beans, Groovy). Upon `readObject()`, the gadget chain executes arbitrary OS commands.

Spring's own Javadoc warns: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context:
HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads HTTP body:
  RemoteInvocation invocation = readRemoteInvocation(request);
  → readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream())
  → ObjectInputStream ois = createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is))
  → createObjectInputStream calls: new CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream(is, getBeanClassLoader(), isAcceptProxyClasses())
  → CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream which extends ObjectInputStream
  → ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() for ClassLoader resolution and resolveProxyClass() for proxy filtering
  → NO ObjectInputFilter is set on the stream
  → doReadRemoteInvocation calls: Object obj = ois.readObject() -- gadgets execute here
  → instanceof check after deserialization too late to prevent gadget execution
evidence.deserialization_chain: HTTP request body → request.getInputStream() → CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream → ois.readObject() → gadget chain executes → instanceof check (too late)
evidence.no_filter: ConfigurableObjectInputStream.java does not call setObjectInputFilter(), does not override resolveClass() with any allowlist (only does ClassLoader-based resolution)

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted Java serialized payload (e.g., Commons Collections gadget chain) via HTTP POST to a Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering remote code execution on the server during deserialization
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint accessible over network; Commons Collections 3.x/4.x or other gadget chain library on server classpath (common in Spring applications)

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization in server-side HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() on HTTP request body without type filtering
findings.reasoning: This method is the server-side deserialization entry point for Spring HTTP Invoker. The call to `ois.readObject()` fully deserializes the incoming byte stream from the HTTP request body with no ObjectInputFilter, no type allowlist, and no class validation BEFORE deserialization occurs. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check happens AFTER deserialization, meaning any malicious gadget chain (e.g., CommonsCollections, Spring Beans) executes during `readObject()` before the type check is reached. The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream` chain provides only ClassLoader resolution and optional proxy class rejection - neither implements an ObjectInputFilter to restrict which classes can be deserialized. `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()` reads directly from `HttpServletRequest.getInputStream()`, making this directly exploitable by any HTTP client that can reach the endpoint. Spring's own Javadoc explicitly warns about this vulnerability.

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from HttpServletRequest.getInputStream(), passes it to readRemoteInvocation(), which calls createObjectInputStream() (returns CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream, no filter) and then doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) which calls ois.readObject() with no type restrictions. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() for ClassLoader selection and resolveProxyClass() for proxy rejection - no ObjectInputFilter is set.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() -> readRemoteInvocation(request, is) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream (no filter) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject() [SINK: unsafe deserialization]
evidence.notes: Spring itself added explicit warnings in Javadoc: 'Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step.' The instanceof check is post-deserialization and does not prevent gadget chain execution.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP POST request with a malicious serialized Java object (gadget chain payload) to the Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering arbitrary code execution on the server during deserialization.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires network access to the HTTP Invoker endpoint (typically a servlet URL). No authentication required if endpoint is publicly accessible. Requires Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans, or another gadget chain library on the server classpath.

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() on HTTP request body without type filtering enables RCE via gadget chains
findings.reasoning: This method calls `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` on data received from HTTP clients (via `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()` which reads from `HttpServletRequest.getInputStream()`). There is no ObjectInputFilter, no allowlist, and no type restriction applied BEFORE deserialization occurs. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check happens AFTER `readObject()` completes, meaning any gadget chain payload (e.g., Commons Collections, Spring Beans) will execute its `readObject()` hooks during deserialization before the type check ever runs. The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream` chain only customizes ClassLoader resolution and proxy class handling - it does NOT restrict which classes can be deserialized. Spring's own Javadoc explicitly warns: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step." This is a textbook unsafe deserialization vulnerability exposing HTTP endpoints to RCE.

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois). The createObjectInputStream returns CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream which extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream which extends ObjectInputStream - no ObjectInputFilter is set anywhere in this chain. The ois.readObject() at line 144 receives the raw HTTP request body from an untrusted client.
evidence.deserialization_chain:
1. HTTP client sends POST request to Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint
2. HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() calls readRemoteInvocation()
3. readRemoteInvocation() passes request.getInputStream() directly to createObjectInputStream()
4. CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream wraps the stream with no type filtering
5. doReadRemoteInvocation() calls ois.readObject() - gadget chain executes HERE
6. instanceof check at line 147 happens AFTER RCE already occurred
evidence.no_filter: ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() and resolveProxyClass() for ClassLoader resolution - it does NOT set an ObjectInputFilter via setObjectInputFilter() or implement any class allowlist/denylist mechanism

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a malicious serialized Java object (gadget chain payload) as the HTTP POST body to a Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering arbitrary code execution on the server during ObjectInputStream.readObject() before the instanceof check
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Network access to the HTTP Invoker endpoint; gadget chain library (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans) on server classpath

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() called on untrusted HTTP request body without type filtering
findings.reasoning: This method calls `ois.readObject()` on data received directly from an HTTP request body (via HttpInvokerServiceExporter which calls `request.getInputStream()`). The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream` used has no ObjectInputFilter configured and provides no class allowlisting - it only handles ClassLoader resolution. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check occurs AFTER deserialization is complete, meaning any gadget chain executes during `readObject()` before the type check. With common gadget chain libraries (Commons Collections, Spring Beans, etc.) on the classpath, this enables unauthenticated RCE. Spring's own Javadoc warns about this: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois). The createObjectInputStream creates a CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream which extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream which extends ObjectInputStream. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() to use a specific ClassLoader - no ObjectInputFilter, no class allowlist. The instanceof check at line 146 happens after readObject() completes, so gadget chains execute before the check.
evidence.deserialization_chain: ObjectInputStream.readObject() -> no filter -> arbitrary class instantiation via gadget chain
evidence.protection_analysis: No ObjectInputFilter set. No class allowlist. Only protection is setAcceptProxyClasses(false) which only prevents proxy class deserialization, not regular class gadget chains.
evidence.spring_warning: Spring's own JavaDoc in HttpInvokerServiceExporter.java line 53-54: 'WARNING: Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step.'
evidence.server_side_entry_point: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() - full HTTP request body from any client

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP POST with a serialized Java gadget chain payload to a Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering arbitrary command execution on the server during deserialization
exploit_payload.prerequisites: A Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint must be exposed (HttpInvokerServiceExporter or SimpleHttpInvokerServiceExporter configured). Gadget chain libraries like Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans, or similar must be on the classpath (very common in Spring applications).

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization in server-side HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() on HTTP request body without type filtering, enabling RCE via gadget chains
findings.reasoning:
The method calls `ois.readObject()` on an ObjectInputStream created from the raw HTTP request body (via HttpServletRequest.getInputStream()). 

1. **No type filtering**: The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream` / `ConfigurableObjectInputStream` chain only provides ClassLoader resolution and proxy class control - no ObjectInputFilter is set, no class allowlist is enforced.

2. **instanceof check is post-exploitation**: The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check happens AFTER deserialization - gadget chain code in `readObject()` executes during deserialization itself, before the type check is reached.

3. **Direct HTTP request body**: `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()` calls `request.getInputStream()` → `createObjectInputStream()` → `doReadRemoteInvocation(ois)`. Any unauthenticated HTTP client can send a crafted payload.

4. **Spring's own warning**: The JavaDoc of HttpInvokerServiceExporter explicitly warns: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

5. **Gadget chain availability**: Spring applications typically have Commons Collections, Spring Beans, or other gadget chain libraries on the classpath, making exploitation straightforward with tools like ysoserial.

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject(). No ObjectInputFilter set anywhere in the chain. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() for ClassLoader lookup - no type restriction.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() in HttpInvokerServiceExporter. Sink: ObjectInputStream.readObject() in RemoteInvocationSerializingExporter.doReadRemoteInvocation() at line 147. No sanitization between source and sink.
evidence.notes: Spring documentation explicitly warns about this vulnerability. CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream wraps ConfigurableObjectInputStream which wraps ObjectInputStream - none of these add type filtering. The only post-deserialization check (instanceof RemoteInvocation) does not prevent gadget chain execution.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP POST containing a ysoserial gadget chain payload to any exposed Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering arbitrary code execution on the server during deserialization
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint must be reachable (not behind authentication or firewall). Common gadget chain libraries (Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans, Groovy) must be on the classpath.

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker - potential RCE from untrusted HTTP clients
findings.reasoning: This method calls `ois.readObject()` on data received from the HTTP request body (via HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() in HttpInvokerServiceExporter). There is no ObjectInputFilter configured, no class allowlist, and no type restriction applied before deserialization. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check happens AFTER deserialization, which means gadget chains can execute during the `readObject()` call before the check. The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream` only provides ClassLoader resolution - it offers no protection against malicious serialized payloads. If gadget chain libraries (Commons Collections, Spring Beans, etc.) are on the classpath, this enables remote code execution from any HTTP client that can send a crafted payload to this endpoint. Spring's own Javadoc explicitly warns: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() -> passes to readRemoteInvocation() -> createObjectInputStream() creates CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream (no filter) -> doReadRemoteInvocation() calls ois.readObject() without any type restriction. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() and resolveProxyClass() for ClassLoader purposes, not for type filtering. No ObjectInputFilter is set.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> request.getInputStream() -> readRemoteInvocation(request, is) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream(is, classLoader, acceptProxyClasses) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject() [SINK - no filter]
evidence.notes: Spring documentation itself acknowledges this risk and warns users to only expose HTTP invoker endpoints between trusted services, not to untrusted clients.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP POST request with a Commons Collections gadget chain payload to the Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering remote code execution during deserialization on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: HTTP Invoker endpoint exposed to untrusted clients; gadget chain libraries (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans) present on server classpath

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() processes untrusted HTTP request body without type filtering, enabling RCE via gadget chains
findings.reasoning:
The `doReadRemoteInvocation()` method calls `ois.readObject()` on an ObjectInputStream that is constructed from an HTTP request's InputStream (via `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.readRemoteInvocation()` → `request.getInputStream()`). 

Key issues:
1. **No ObjectInputFilter**: The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream` → `ConfigurableObjectInputStream` chain does NOT configure any `ObjectInputFilter` or override `resolveClass()` with type allowlisting. The only security measure is `acceptProxyClasses` which only blocks proxy class deserialization.
2. **Type check happens too late**: The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check occurs AFTER `readObject()` returns. Java deserialization executes all `readObject()` and constructor methods of the deserialized object graph during `readObject()` - this is where gadget chains execute.
3. **Direct HTTP request body deserialization**: `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()` calls `readRemoteInvocation(request)` which calls `request.getInputStream()` and passes it directly to `doReadRemoteInvocation()`.
4. **No authentication by default**: The HTTP endpoint is a `HttpRequestHandler` that processes all incoming requests.

Spring themselves warn in the Javadoc: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

With gadget chain libraries like Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Commons BeanUtils, or Spring Beans on the classpath (which is highly likely in any Spring application), an attacker can send a crafted serialized payload to achieve arbitrary RCE.

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() calls readRemoteInvocation(request) which calls request.getInputStream() and passes it through createObjectInputStream() (which creates a CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream with no type filter) to doReadRemoteInvocation() which calls ois.readObject() unconditionally. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() to use a custom ClassLoader - there is no ObjectInputFilter, no allowlist check, no denylist.
evidence.notes: Spring Javadoc explicitly warns: 'Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step. As a consequence, do not expose HTTP invoker endpoints to untrusted clients.' This is CVE-2016-1000027 (Spring HTTP Invoker unsafe deserialization).

exploit_payload.prerequisites: Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint exposed to network; gadget chain library (Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Spring Beans, or other) on classpath - standard for Spring applications

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker - ObjectInputStream.readObject() on untrusted HTTP request body with no type filtering
findings.reasoning: This method calls `ois.readObject()` on an ObjectInputStream created directly from the HTTP request body (via `HttpServletRequest.getInputStream()` in `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()`). There is no ObjectInputFilter configured, no type allowlist, and no denylist. The only protection is the `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check which occurs AFTER deserialization - gadget chain payloads (e.g., Commons Collections, Spring Beans) would execute their malicious code during `readObject()` before the type check. The `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream` only provides ClassLoader-based class resolution and proxy class control - neither implements any security filtering. The Spring framework itself warns in HttpInvokerServiceExporter Javadoc: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject(). The ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() and resolveProxyClass() for ClassLoader purposes - no ObjectInputFilter is set. The instanceof check after readObject() is too late to prevent gadget chain execution.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() -> ObjectInputStream (CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream) -> readObject() -> instanceof check (too late)
evidence.notes: Spring itself acknowledges this vulnerability and recommends not exposing HTTP invoker to untrusted clients. Known affected CVEs include CVE-2016-1000027 (Spring Framework HTTP Invoker deserialization). Gadget chains from Commons Collections 3.x/4.x, Commons Beanutils, and Spring Beans are exploitable.

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization on HTTP-received data in AbstractHttpInvokerRequestExecutor - client-side deserialization of HTTP response with no type filtering
findings.reasoning: The `doReadRemoteInvocationResult` method in AbstractHttpInvokerRequestExecutor calls `ois.readObject()` on an ObjectInputStream that wraps the HTTP response body. There is no ObjectInputFilter or type allowlist configured. The `instanceof RemoteInvocationResult` check occurs AFTER deserialization, meaning any gadget chain (e.g., Commons Collections, Spring Beans) will execute during `readObject()` before the type check. The ObjectInputStream is created via `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream` which extends `ConfigurableObjectInputStream` which extends `ObjectInputStream` - none of these configure any ObjectInputFilter. A MITM attacker or malicious server can send a crafted serialized payload to achieve RCE on the client.

evidence.code_context: AbstractHttpInvokerRequestExecutor.java lines 270-298: The readRemoteInvocationResult method reads from HTTP response InputStream, creates CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream (no type filter), and calls doReadRemoteInvocationResult which calls ois.readObject() with no protection. ConfigurableObjectInputStream.java shows no ObjectInputFilter is set - only ClassLoader resolution and proxy class control. The class hierarchy: CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream -> ConfigurableObjectInputStream -> ObjectInputStream, none set an ObjectInputFilter.
evidence.notes: Spring's own Javadoc warns: 'Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step.' This is the client-side counterpart - if the server is compromised or a MITM attack is possible, the client is vulnerable.

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker - doReadRemoteInvocation() calls ObjectInputStream.readObject() on untrusted HTTP request data without type filtering, enabling RCE via gadget chains
findings.reasoning: The doReadRemoteInvocation() method calls ObjectInputStream.readObject() on a stream that originates from HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() (in HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest()). The ObjectInputStream is created as a CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream which extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream (which extends ObjectInputStream) - neither of these apply any ObjectInputFilter or type allowlist. The instanceof check for RemoteInvocation happens AFTER deserialization, meaning any gadget chain (Commons Collections, Spring Beans, etc.) that executes during readObject() will run before the type check. The Spring framework itself warns in the Javadoc: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from request.getInputStream() -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) creates CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream with no type filter -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) calls ois.readObject() with no ObjectInputFilter. ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() to use a specific ClassLoader and resolveProxyClass() to control proxy class acceptance - no allowlist filtering of classes.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() -> CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream (no ObjectInputFilter) -> ObjectInputStream.readObject() -> gadget chain execution -> instanceof check (too late)
evidence.notes: Spring explicitly documents this as a security warning in the class Javadoc. No ObjectInputFilter is set on the ObjectInputStream. The only protection is the optional acceptProxyClasses=false setting, which only prevents proxy class deserialization but not non-proxy gadget chains like Commons Collections.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a malicious serialized Java object as the HTTP body to a Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint; the server deserializes it via ObjectInputStream.readObject() without type filtering, executing a gadget chain that achieves RCE
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint exposed to untrusted clients AND gadget chain library (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x) on the classpath

findings.description: Server-side unsafe Java deserialization of HTTP request body in Spring HTTP Invoker - RCE with gadget chains
findings.reasoning:
This method calls `ois.readObject()` with no ObjectInputFilter, no type allowlist, and no class name validation before deserialization. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check happens AFTER deserialization, meaning all gadget chain execution from `readObject()` has already occurred by the time the type check runs. 

The ObjectInputStream is created via `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream` → `ConfigurableObjectInputStream` → `ObjectInputStream`. None of these classes implement an `ObjectInputFilter` or override `resolveClass()` with any security constraints - `resolveClass()` only resolves classes via a custom ClassLoader, providing no security boundary.

The call chain is: `HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest(HttpServletRequest)` → `readRemoteInvocation(request)` → `readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream())` → `createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(...))` → `doReadRemoteInvocation(ois)` → `ois.readObject()`.

This means any HTTP client that can reach the `/invoker` endpoint can send a serialized gadget chain (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x, Commons Beanutils, Spring itself) and achieve Remote Code Execution. Spring's own Javadoc on `HttpInvokerServiceExporter` warns: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() reads from HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() and passes directly to ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no filtering. The complete chain: handleRequest -> readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois) -> ois.readObject(). ConfigurableObjectInputStream only overrides resolveClass() to use a custom ClassLoader, not to restrict class types. No ObjectInputFilter is set anywhere in the chain.
evidence.deserialization_analysis: CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream which extends ObjectInputStream. The resolveClass() override only calls ClassUtils.forName() with a custom classloader - no allowlist/denylist. No setObjectInputFilter() call anywhere in the chain. The instanceof check on line 147 is a post-deserialization check - gadget chains execute during readObject() before this check.
evidence.javadoc_warning: HttpInvokerServiceExporter Javadoc explicitly states: 'WARNING: Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step. As a consequence, do not expose HTTP invoker endpoints to untrusted clients but rather just between your own services.'

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted serialized Java object (gadget chain) as the HTTP POST body to the Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint, triggering RCE when the server calls ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no type filtering
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The HTTP Invoker endpoint must be network-accessible to the attacker. No authentication required by default. Gadget chain library (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x) must be on the server classpath - Spring applications commonly include these.
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via unsafe Java deserialization

findings.description: Unsafe Java deserialization in Spring HTTP Invoker server-side handler - ObjectInputStream.readObject() called on untrusted HTTP request body with no type filtering
findings.reasoning: The `doReadRemoteInvocation()` method calls `ois.readObject()` on an ObjectInputStream that was created from the HTTP request body (`request.getInputStream()`). There is no ObjectInputFilter, no type allowlist, and no class restriction configured on the underlying `CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream`/`ConfigurableObjectInputStream`. The `instanceof RemoteInvocation` check occurs AFTER deserialization - by the time this check runs, any gadget chain constructors and readObject() methods have already been invoked. An attacker can send a malicious serialized payload (e.g., using Commons Collections, Spring Beans gadget chains) to achieve RCE. Spring itself warns in the Javadoc of both `HttpInvokerServiceExporter` and `SimpleHttpInvokerServiceExporter`: "Be aware of vulnerabilities due to unsafe Java deserialization: Manipulated input streams could lead to unwanted code execution on the server during the deserialization step."

evidence.code_context: HttpInvokerServiceExporter.handleRequest() calls readRemoteInvocation(request) -> readRemoteInvocation(request, request.getInputStream()) -> createObjectInputStream(decorateInputStream(request, is)) -> doReadRemoteInvocation(ois). The CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream extends ConfigurableObjectInputStream which extends ObjectInputStream - only provides ClassLoader resolution and optional proxy class control, but NO ObjectInputFilter/type allowlist. The instanceof check at line 149 happens after readObject() completes.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request body -> HttpServletRequest.getInputStream() -> CodebaseAwareObjectInputStream -> ObjectInputStream.readObject() -> gadget chain execution -> instanceof check (too late)
evidence.notes: Spring's own documentation warns about this vulnerability. The fix would be to configure an ObjectInputFilter or use a class allowlist.  Known exploitable with Commons Collections 3.x/4.x gadget chains.

exploit_payload.prerequisites: Target application exposes Spring HTTP Invoker endpoint; gadget chain library (e.g., Commons Collections 3.x/4.x) must be on classpath
